sentences.text: My name is Arnold Wininger.
I used to be Arnold Weininger.
OK, now I'm spelling it W-I-N-I-N-G-E-R.
Originally, it used to be W-E-I-N-I-N-G-E-R.
Yes I do.
My Hebrew name is Aahron Ben Shlomo.
No, I can spell it in English.
I think it's aleph but easier.
A-A-H-R-O-N.
Aahron Ben, the son of Shlomo, S-H-L-O-M-O.
No.
A lot of people call me Arnie.
Instead of Arnold, the abbreviation is Arnie.
So I'm known today-- when my friends, they call me Arnie.
I was born on December 29, 1926.
I'll be, in another month, almost, I'll be 70.
December 29, so a month and a week, a month and a half, something like this, I'll be 70.
I was born in Leipzig, Germany.
Well, I remember it was a very thriving, great Jewish community.
We had several synagogues, anywhere from reform to orthodox.
And it was a very cultural city.
If you've taken history, it's the home of the famous Gewandhaus, one of the great concert halls of Europe.
It's the birthplace of Johann Sebastian Bach.
As a matter of fact, he was the Cantor at Thomas-- St.
Thomas Church, or Thomas [NON-ENGLISH].
It was also the seat of the annual international fair, [SPEAKING GERMAN] where they used to come from all over the world to Leipzig.
The expositions, like a commercial fair.
And it also had, I think, the largest and the greatest railway station in the world.
This is what I remember about the-- The Jewish community was-- we had organizations.
We had a sports club.
We had even a big sports campus.
So really, there was a thriving Jewish life in Leipzig.
Well, there was anything from [SPEAKING HEBREW] to the Betar.
And then you had things like I belonged to, like Maccabi, that's like a scout organization.
And so you had all kinds of Jewish activities.
I don't remember hearing Hadassah for the women, but I remember my mother used to belong to an organization called Ruth, which could have been, probably, the forerunner of Hadassah.
But there was everything.
I'm not too familiar with the women's organizations because we were just two boys, but you had everything in Leipzig.
Yes.
I lived on, it was called Gneisenau Strasse.
G-N-E-I-S-E-N-A-U.
Then Strasse, S-T-R-A-S-S-E.
Gneisenau, by the way, was a famous German general.
I think he's sometimes in some war.
Not World War I, I think before World War I.
As a matter of fact, I was there in 1988.
I saw the house we lived in.
It was still there.
Yeah, we played out in the streets like anybody else.
Don't forget, the Nazis did not come to power until I was about seven.
So we played out on the streets.
We played in the backyard.
It was a little small.
We lived in an apartment house, so it was only very little, small.
But later on, when I was old enough to belong to the Jewish organizations, we had a beautiful sports field out there.
And I remember, as a matter of fact, as a kid in the beginning, I was a ball boy.
The older people used to play tennis, and I used to go collect the tennis balls for them.
They used to give me $0.10, which was a lot of money.
But that's-- I, at present, belonged to Maccabis there, which was similar to a scout or scouting organization.
I had friends, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] neighbors, nothing special.
Mostly Jewish.
I don't recall any non-Jewish friends.
Usually, in those days already, and we lived in a predominantly Jewish neighborhood.
It wasn't all Jewish, so we played with other kids.
I don't remember anymore, but the friends were mostly Jewish.
No, no my family considered itself Jewish.
We were orthodox.
Not strictly orthodox.
We were not Hasidim.
We were orthodox.
But my parents considered themselves so-called Ostjuden, or Eastern or Polish Jews.
In the sense that the German Jews actually did not accept us as one of theirs.
I never considered myself a German Jew, even though I was born in Germany, my mother tongue is German.
We were never-- we were considered East European, or they used to call it Polish Jews, because my parents emigrated from Eastern Europe in the '20s.
And the German Jews, they didn't mix.
As a matter of fact, they had their own synagogues.
And I don't recall having any interaction with real German Jews.
Maybe there were some kids in school.
I forgot already.
But generally speaking, we used to hang around mostly with kids from Eastern Europe.
Their parents were from Eastern Europe.
So the only ones-- later on, Hitler taught us that all Jews are alike.
He didn't care if they came from Germany.
They were born in Germany, or born in Warsaw, or Kolomea, or Lemburg whatever it is.
A Jew is a Jew.
Not that I know of.
I'm sure that there must have been a social conflicts, but I was too young to really get involved in that.
I don't know.
I have no idea.
Well, yes.
My father, Solomon Weininger, was born in a place called Sniatyn.
S-N-I-A-T-Y-N.
Something like that.
Which when he was born it was Austria.
He only spoke German.
And Yiddish, naturally, and Hebrew.
He could read Hebrew.
I don't think he spoke Hebrew, what they called modern Hebrew.
And he served in the Austria-Hungarian Imperial Army.
He rose to the rank of staff sergeant, or quartermaster.
He fought in World War I for Austria with Germany, naturally.
Austria was an ally of Germany.
He was wounded twice.
He had five decorations, which when he was arrested by the Gestapo, he took it with him.
What they say in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], did not help a bit.
Didn't mean anything.
He immigrated-- I don't know, probably-- let's see, World War II was over in 19-- World War I was over 1918.
I would say probably 1920, something like that, he came to Leipzig.
And he first was in the wholesale egg business.
Later on, he went-- he went into the wholesale fur business, like, I would say, 75% of the Jewish in Leipzig were in the fur business.
Leipzig was, in those days, the fur capital of the world.
I think the other place was London and New York.
But in Europe, anything to do with furs, Leipzig was the place.
He was a hard working man.
I don't know.
I really don't know.
We never-- he never talked about it.
But he was an educated man.
Probably went through a gymnasium.
I'm sure he went through a gymnasium but we never talked about it.
He never told us about it because we were too young, really.
He was well educated.
Yes, yes, yes.
As a matter of fact, I have a-- I have a picture.
I don't have it with me.
I will try to get it to you.
I have a picture what he looked like.
He was a handsome man.
I remember he wore a black mustache, and I remember he smoked cigarettes.
Otherwise, he was a hard working man and dedicated to his family.
To the family.
And I really did not have a chance to be too much with him because he was always working.
And later on, the recreation facilities, you really couldn't do too much.
You couldn't go camping or stuff like that.
It just wasn't done.
But he made sure that we had a good, Jewish education.
That we went to cheder after school, and we observed all the holidays, and went to shul and things like that.
So that's my father.
My mother, her name was [NON-ENGLISH] or Rachel today.
Rachel.
Her maiden name was Ucker, U-C-K-E-R.
She was born in a place called Washkowitz.
Later on, in Romanian it became known as Vascauti.
It was not too far from Chernowitz.
Chernowitz, C-H-E-R-N-O-W-I-T-Z, I believe.
Washkowitz was, I would say W-A-S-H-K-O-W-I-T-Z.
Something like that.
You know?
And she was very, highly intelligent, motivated woman.
Great believer in the arts.
As a matter of fact, she was a maven on the history of the royal houses of Europe.
The Hapsburgs.
She could tell us that Prince so and so married princess from here, and et cetera, et cetera.
She used to take us to the opera.
She used to read us from the great German poet, Schiller.
She loved Schiller.
And even to this day, I love Schiller.
I remember some of the poetry.
She used to read us poetry.
And I remember one time, Jewish were not supposed to go to the theater, to the concert.
Somehow, since we didn't look Jewish, so to speak, we were able to sneak in and listen to a concert because she was so devoted.
Probably would be arrested.
I don't know.
It was in the early stages.
I don't know what would have happened.
But I guess she was brave enough to take a chance of going to the concert and this-- we went out--
Probably 1936 or '37.
I know it was before Kristallnacht.
Before 1938.
So you know, you could-- there was a little bit of a leeway, I'm sure.
Yes.
I remember.
She was a beautiful woman.
As a matter of fact, when I came to the United States, in New York, somehow I knew somebody that was a distant relative, or came from the same town.
And he told me that he used to try to get my mother's attention.
He said she was the most beautiful girl in town.
No.
I have a picture.
I have a-- one picture of her, which I will give to you so you can see it.
She was a very educated, cultured, devoted mother.
She was a-- I mean, she worked in Vienna.
She was a dental nurse.
So she liked medicine, but I think, above all else, she-- I don't know the level of education that she had.
Don't forget, in those days, women did not achieve the same level of education.
You didn't send a girl to university in those days.
But she was an avid reader.
And like I said, she knew her way around.
She was, to a certain degree, street smart.
She knew, even in tough times, how to get an extra piece of bread, buy a ration card from somebody.
She knew-- that's why I said to myself, somehow my mother must have survived.
But apparently, she didn't.
I remember my grandparents vaguely.
I remember 1932, we took a trip first to Poland.
My father's part of it became Poland.
And I met my paternal grandfather, Yehuda.
I don't know-- I forgot my grandmother's name.
I don't even know my grandfather on my mother's side.
But his name was Yehuda.
And we met there.
I was too young to remember him.
Then later on, we went over to Washkovitz, and I met my maternal grandfather and his second wife.
What were their names?
I don't know.
I forgot.
I have no idea.
Oh, his name was Gershon, and he was-- I really don't know what he did for a living.
I have no idea.
But I know one thing.
He was inseparable from what's called the Vishnitzer Rebbe.
And as a matter of fact, somehow, we were distant, distant cousins to the Vishnitzer Rebbe.
Can you tell me about him a little bit?
What, the Vishnitzer Rebbe?
I have no idea.
The Vishnitzer Rebbe was one of the great wonder rabbis of its time.
I remember one year, mother took us to Karlsbad.
Later on, you'll see that picture of him with the Vishnitzer Rebbe.
Karlsbad and Marienbad were two of those resorts where the Jews from Eastern Europe used to go.
It was in Czechoslovakia, up in what's called the Sudetenland.
Later on, it became part of Germany.
And they used to go there.
And you had to go to see a doctor.
He prescribed.
You told him what ached you, you know?
In those days, everybody had some kind of aches.
And he used to prescribe certain drinks.
They had these health spas.
Certain wells.
I once tasted it, and I don't want to say on camera what it tasted.
So one day, we came there.
And she wanted the rabbi, the wonder rabbi to bless us children, the boys.
I forget.
She could hardly talk to him because the wonder rabbi doesn't talk to women.
She had to talk to the rabbi's wife.
So that's all I know.
I know he was a very revered man.
If the rabbi said so, this was it.
He was a big, big, heavy set guy.
He's similar to what-- in New York, we had the-- what's his name-- Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson or Rabbi Soloveitchik.
Rabbi Soloveitchik is much more modern.
But you still have them in Israel.
These wonder-- these rabbis who Hasidim come, and whatever he says, they look for his advice.
And whatever he says, that's it.
That's all I remember about him.
I never-- I have no idea.
I think, as a matter of fact, the dynasty is still around.
I think the name was Hager, or something like that.
I don't even know his name.
But I think his grandson-- the Vishnitzer Rebbe dynasty is still around in Israel today.
I didn't see them.
I didn't go and look for them.
But I've heard, I think, his grandson, he's there.
Still preaching, and davening.
Probably lives in [INAUDIBLE].
No.
I would say my parents-- I'm just guessing-- must got married around 1923, or '24, something like that.
But I have no idea when they got married.
I'm sure they got married in Leipzig.
I know that.
Yes.
I had a brother.
His name was David.
He was born on August 12, 1928.
He was around two years younger than I.
Very smart boy.
He was smarter than I.
Better looking than I.
He had more friends.
He was very outgoing.
He was the popular boy with all the kids and everything else.
And he remained with my mother, OK?
So whatever happened to my mother happened to him.
Oh, yes.
I remember the holidays we celebrated.
And I also remember Pesach.
Pesach was a tradition.
We used to go over to my uncle's house.
His name was Abich Frenkel.
He was a half brother to my mother.
See, my-- Gershon Ucker, I think my mother, [NON-ENGLISH] is probably part of even the second or the third marriage.
I don't know exactly.
But from the first marriage or second marriage, he had other sons and daughters.
And two of them lived in Leipzig.
And one of them, it was tradition that we used to go to him for Pesach.
As a matter of fact, the other guest at the house all the time was Dr.
Joseph Borg.
As I'm sure Israelis would know, he was the minister of Religious Affairs, and his son today is, I think, the head of the Jewish agency, or whatever it is.
Borg, I sat at the same table as Joseph Borg.
I was a kid.
If I reminded him I was there, he wouldn't remember it.
But if I mentioned my uncle's name, he certainly would know who I'm talking about, because they were very-- he was a professor at the [NON-ENGLISH] school gymnasium where I went to.
He was very well known.
So Dr.
Joseph Borg was also at the holidays.
And I remember Hanukkah, and Purim, and Rosh Hashanah, and Yom Kippur.
We'd all go to shul in those days, the men used to wear high-- high top hats and everything else.
I surely remember the holidays, yes.
Who was doing the cooking?
Well, my mother did the cooking.
In the beginning, I remember I think 1931, I think we had the lady come into the house.
I don't know if she did the cooking.
But we had a live-in maid.
No.
We used to call her Fraulein, which is Miss.
We always used to-- I forgot her name.
Maybe I knew her name.
I have no idea.
No, I don't think she was Jewish.
But later on, after 1933 or whenever the racial laws came out in Nuremberg with the Nazis, you couldn't even have-- you could not employ a non-Jewish housekeeper.
But we didn't have a-- [NON-ENGLISH] we didn't have anybody.
So I remember my mother laying out, making challah.
OK, and I remember her-- for spaghetti, she used to take the dough and just spread it over the table.
So I very well-- going to get a chicken, had to show her-- everything was strictly kosher.
And fish.
She made her own gefilte fish.
Not like today.
There was no Manischewitz to open up, the boxes.
She did all this cooking.
She was a very good cook.
Oh my.
I don't remember my favorite dish anymore.
I think I liked gefilte fish, and I like sweets to this day.
I have no recollection of a favorite dish.
Not that they affected us directly-- affected me directly as a kid.
I was-- but I remember in school, I have some memories.
Because the first four years Hitler came to power, I was in public school.
And two things I remember.
I could not go with the children for swimming lessons.
Because as a Jew, I was polluting the pool.
I remember that.
And I have to admit, I'm ashamed to say, to this day, I don't know how to swim.
And all my kids born here were lifeguards at one time or another.
Somehow I developed a fear of the water.
That's the one thing.
The other thing I remember, in school, I was always very good in the German language and geography.
I was not the greatest in math and sciences, but I managed OK.
And I remember one day, we had some kind of an essay to do.
And the teacher used to-- I think I was the best in class.
And the teacher used me as an example.
Pointed the kids to me and says, look, this foreigner knows your language better than you.
I was a foreigner.
And I came home that day, and I asked my mother.
I said, mother, am I a foreigner?
I mean, I only knew German.
I was born there, and that's the only language I knew, OK?
I never considered myself a foreigner.
I was one of the boys like the rest of them.
And she said, no, you're not a foreigner.
But you're not a German, you're not-- you're not a goy.
And for them, you're something different.
So I remember these are the things that affected me as a kid.
I'll never forget that I was a foreigner in school.
What year was this?
Oh, I would say 1934 or '35.
Something like that.
The Nazis had just come-- the Nazis came to power in 1933.
And they developed the Hitler Youth and all the different things.
Which naturally we didn't participate in it.
They used to give out free milk to all the kids except-- there were some other Jewish kids.
I was not the only kid in school.
Jewish kids didn't get the milk because they said their parents have enough money to buy for them.
So there's little innuendos.
Little discriminations.
Subtle.
I remember my class, we had about four or five Jewish kids in class.
The class, maybe 20, 25.
We're very good-- by the way, I have to admit it, the German education system was excellent.
School was serious business.
Not like, unfortunately, the United States.
School was a very serious.
